model_narrative: This is a regional agricultural planning model for a farming zone in North-East Brazil. It chooses the activity level of every production, processing, transport and accounting variable in the zone. The plan is scored by a single accounting variable that measures the combined consumer and producer surplus, the overall farm income, and the goal is to make that surplus as large as possible.

sets.name: V
sets.doc: the indices of the decision variables of the regional farm LP, one per production, processing, transport and accounting activity in the zone

sets.name: E
sets.doc: the indices of the equations of the regional farm LP, each one a single resource, balance or accounting relationship

sets.name: Vfree
sets.doc: the subset of variable indices that are free variables ranging over all reals with no bounds, including the consumer-producer surplus accounting variable

sets.name: Vbfree
sets.doc: the subset of variable indices that are bounded-free, ranging from zero up to a finite per-variable upper bound

params.name: A
params.doc: the sparse constraint coefficient on variable v in equation e; only the nonzero entries are stored, so an equation involves only the variables that actually appear in it

params.name: rhs
params.doc: the right-hand side constant of each equation, the resource limit, requirement or balance target it is compared against

params.name: sense
params.doc: the relational direction of each equation given as a code: -1 means the equation is a less-than-or-equal upper-limit constraint, 0 means it is an equality balance, and 1 means it is a greater-than-or-equal lower-requirement constraint

params.name: vub
params.doc: the finite upper bound for each bounded-free variable, in the variable's own activity units

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the activity level of each decision variable; free variables in Vfree range over all reals, bounded-free variables in Vbfree range from zero to their upper bound, and every other variable is nonnegative

constraints.name: A.keys()
constraints.intent: For each equation in the model, take the weighted sum of the decision variables that appear in that equation, each weighted by its coefficient in that equation, and relate that sum to the equation's right-hand side value. The direction of the relationship is set per equation by its sense: when the sense marks it as an upper limit the weighted sum must stay at or below the right-hand side, when the sense marks it as a lower requirement the weighted sum must stay at or above the right-hand side, and otherwise the weighted sum must equal the right-hand side exactly.

query: There's a proposal on the table to keep the zone's three heaviest activities — x[350], x[384] and x[378] — to a combined level of no more than 300000. What does the optimizer return for the western-zone surplus with that joint ceiling in place?

query: We're looking at a concentration guard for the plan's big four: x[350] should account for at most a quarter of the sum of x[350], x[384], x[378] and x[360] taken together. Plug that in and walk me through where farm income lands.

query: Engineering is pitching a uniform throttle across the small bounded block: each of the activities x[284] through x[295] held to at most 550 units. What's the new surplus when every member of that block obeys the same 550 cap?

query: Let's try a two-part package: throttle x[350] to 100000, and — so the workload doesn't simply pile onto x[360] — hold x[360] to no more than 100000 as well. Treat it as one scenario; what surplus does the model report?

query: Five activities — x[276], x[287], x[288], x[289] and x[293] — sit at exactly zero in the recommended plan, which smells like wasted capacity. Couldn't we just require those five to contribute at least 3000 units between them? What's keeping the model from making that move?
expected_pyomo: model.floor_idle5 = Constraint(expr=model.x[276] + model.x[287] + model.x[288] + model.x[289] + model.x[293] >= 3000)

query: Four members of the 272-283 block — x[272], x[273], x[282] and x[283] — are all maxed out while x[274] idles well below its bound. Shouldn't we rebalance: cap those four at 18000 combined and require x[274] to carry at least 4500? What's the tradeoff the optimizer is making by not doing that?
expected_pyomo:
model.cap_block_four = Constraint(expr=model.x[272] + model.x[273] + model.x[282] + model.x[283] <= 18000)
model.floor_x274 = Constraint(expr=model.x[274] >= 4500)
